1_immunity_and_immune_system.q: Which immunity is immediate and non-specific?
1_immunity_and_immune_system.l: Innate immunity provides immediate non-specific protection.
Immunity is resistance to diseases.

a: Cells, tissues and molecules that resist infection
1_immunity_and_immune_system.l: The immune system mediates resistance to infections.

1_immunity_and_immune_system.q: Which immunity develops slowly and is highly specific?
1_immunity_and_immune_system.l: Adaptive immunity develops slowly and is highly specific.
Cell-mediated immunity is mediated by T lymphocytes.
Humoral immunity is mediated by B lymphocytes.

1_immunity_and_immune_system.q: Which is an innate immune cell?
1_immunity_and_immune_system.l: Neutrophils are major innate immune cells.

1_immunity_and_immune_system.q: Which receptor type is used by innate immunity?
1_immunity_and_immune_system.l: Innate immunity uses pattern recognition receptors (PRRs).
PAMPs are pathogen-associated molecular patterns.

1_immunity_and_immune_system.q: Which receptors are used by adaptive immunity?
1_immunity_and_immune_system.l: Adaptive immunity uses BCRs and TCRs.
PRR stands for pattern recognition receptor.
TLR-2 recognizes peptidoglycan from Gram-positive bacteria.
TLR-4 recognizes LPS from Gram-negative bacteria.
TLR-5 recognizes bacterial flagellin.
TLR-7 and TLR-8 recognize viral ssRNA.
TLR-3 recognizes viral dsRNA.
NF-kB is Nuclear Factor kappa-light-chain-enhancer of activated B cells.
NF-kB regulates gene transcription.
NF-kB regulates both innate and adaptive immunity.

1_immunity_and_immune_system.q: Which barrier is the first line of defense?
1_immunity_and_immune_system.l: Skin is a major physical barrier.

1_immunity_and_immune_system.q: Which enzyme in tears is antimicrobial?
1_immunity_and_immune_system.l: Lysozyme destroys bacterial cell walls.

1_immunity_and_immune_system.q: Which innate cell kills virus-infected cells?
1_immunity_and_immune_system.l: NK cells kill virus-infected cells.
IFN-alpha and IFN-beta inhibit viral replication.

1_immunity_and_immune_system.q: What is the role of APCs?
1_immunity_and_immune_system.l: APCs present antigen to T lymphocytes.
Dendritic cells are professional APCs.
Bone marrow and thymus are primary lymphoid organs.
Lymph nodes, spleen and MALT are secondary lymphoid organs.
B cells develop in the bone marrow.
T cells develop in the thymus.

1_immunity_and_immune_system.q: Which lymph node region activates B cells?
1_immunity_and_immune_system.l: B cells are activated in the cortex.

1_immunity_and_immune_system.q: Which lymph node region activates T cells?
1_immunity_and_immune_system.l: T cells are activated in the paracortex.

1_immunity_and_immune_system.q: Which organ responds to blood-borne antigens?
1_immunity_and_immune_system.l: The spleen responds to blood-borne antigens.

1_immunity_and_immune_system.q: Which disease can cause splenic dysfunction?
1_immunity_and_immune_system.l: Sickle cell disease causes functional asplenia.

1_immunity_and_immune_system.q: Asplenic patients are susceptible to which organisms?
1_immunity_and_immune_system.l: Asplenia predisposes to encapsulated organisms.

1_immunity_and_immune_system.q: What blood finding is seen after splenectomy?
1_immunity_and_immune_system.l: Howell-Jolly bodies are seen after splenectomy.
Myeloid cells form platelets, RBCs and granulocytes.
Lymphoid lineage forms T cells, B cells and NK cells.

1_immunity_and_immune_system.q: Which antibody binds Fc receptors on neutrophils?
1_immunity_and_immune_system.l: Neutrophils have Fc receptors that bind IgG.

1_immunity_and_immune_system.q: Neutropenia increases risk of which infection?
1_immunity_and_immune_system.l: Pseudomonas is a classic neutropenic infection.

1_immunity_and_immune_system.q: Fc receptors are found on which cell?
1_immunity_and_immune_system.l: Fc receptors are present on neutrophils, macrophages, NK cells and eosinophils.

1_immunity_and_immune_system.q: What happens before Fc receptor binding?
1_immunity_and_immune_system.l: IgG first coats pathogens before binding Fc receptors.

1_immunity_and_immune_system.q: Which antibodies bind eosinophil Fc receptors?
1_immunity_and_immune_system.l: Eosinophils have Fc receptors for IgG and IgE.

2_acute_inflammatory_response.l: Inflammation is the body's first response to injury, infection, or tissue damage.
Neutrophils predominate in acute inflammation.
Macrophages predominate in chronic inflammation.

2_acute_inflammatory_response.q: Pus is rich in which cell?
2_acute_inflammatory_response.l: Pyogenic infections produce neutrophil-rich pus.
Granulomatous inflammation is macrophage predominant.
Margination is the first step of leukocyte recruitment.
Sialyl-Lewis X mediates leukocyte rolling.
Integrin CD18 mediates firm adhesion.
PECAM-1 mediates transmigration (diapedesis).
Chemotaxis is directed migration toward inflammatory mediators.
IL-8 is a major neutrophil chemotactic factor.
C5a is a potent chemotactic factor.
Histamine causes vasodilation and increased permeability.
PGE₂ mediates pain, fever and vasodilation.
PGI₂ causes vasodilation and inhibits platelet aggregation.
TXA₂ promotes platelet aggregation and vasoconstriction.
LTB4 is a potent neutrophil chemotactic factor.
These leukotrienes cause bronchoconstriction.
TNF-α and IL-1 cause fever and endothelial activation.
IL-6 stimulates acute phase reactants such as CRP.
IgM is the first antibody produced.
IgG crosses the placenta.
IgA provides mucosal immunity.
IgE mediates allergy and parasitic defense.

2_acute_inflammatory_response.q: Which immunoglobulin acts as a B-cell receptor?
2_acute_inflammatory_response.l: IgD functions as a B-cell receptor.
IgG is a major opsonin.
Respiratory burst generates reactive oxygen species.
NADPH oxidase forms superoxide radicals.
Superoxide dismutase forms H₂O₂.
MPO generates HOCl (bleach).
HOCl is the most potent neutrophil bactericidal agent.
LAD I is caused by CD18 deficiency.
LAD I causes defective adhesion.

2_acute_inflammatory_response.q: Classic neonatal finding in LAD I?
2_acute_inflammatory_response.l: Delayed umbilical cord separation is classic.

2_acute_inflammatory_response.q: LAD II is due to deficiency of?
2_acute_inflammatory_response.l: LAD II is caused by Sialyl-Lewis X deficiency.
LAD II causes defective rolling.
Chediak-Higashi is due to LYST mutation.
Defective lysosomal trafficking causes defective phagolysosome formation.
Albinism is characteristic of Chediak-Higashi syndrome.

2_acute_inflammatory_response.q: CGD is caused by deficiency of?
2_acute_inflammatory_response.l: CGD results from NADPH oxidase deficiency.
CGD patients cannot generate superoxide radicals.
CGD causes recurrent catalase-positive infections.
DHR test is abnormal in CGD.
NBT test is negative in CGD.

2_acute_inflammatory_response.q: MPO deficiency causes defective production of?
2_acute_inflammatory_response.l: MPO deficiency impairs HOCl production.
Respiratory burst remains normal in MPO deficiency.
MPO deficiency increases susceptibility to Candida.
NBT test is positive in MPO deficiency.
DHR test is normal in MPO deficiency.

3_compliments_and_cytokine.q: How many plasma proteins are present in the complement system?
3_compliments_and_cytokine.l: The complement system consists of more than 20 plasma proteins.

3_compliments_and_cytokine.q: In what form are complement proteins present in serum?
3_compliments_and_cytokine.l: Complement proteins circulate in inactive form until activated.

3_compliments_and_cytokine.q: The complement system functions in which types of immunity?
3_compliments_and_cytokine.l: Complement functions in both innate and adaptive immunity.

3_compliments_and_cytokine.q: How is the complement system activated?
3_compliments_and_cytokine.l: Complement is activated through an enzymatic cascade with amplification.

3_compliments_and_cytokine.q: Which of the following is a function of the complement system?
3_compliments_and_cytokine.l: Complement promotes opsonization, inflammation, chemotaxis, and cell lysis.

3_compliments_and_cytokine.q: How many pathways activate the complement system?
3_compliments_and_cytokine.l: Three pathways activate complement: Classical, Alternative, and Lectin.

3_compliments_and_cytokine.q: Which pathways activate complement?
3_compliments_and_cytokine.l: The three complement pathways are Classical, Alternative, and Lectin.

3_compliments_and_cytokine.q: All complement pathways converge at the cleavage of which components?
3_compliments_and_cytokine.l: All three pathways converge at C3 and C5 cleavage.

3_compliments_and_cytokine.q: Which complement pathway requires antibodies?
3_compliments_and_cytokine.l: The classical pathway is the only pathway that requires antibodies.

3_compliments_and_cytokine.q: Which antibodies activate the classical pathway?
3_compliments_and_cytokine.l: The classical pathway is activated by IgG or IgM antigen-antibody complexes.

3_compliments_and_cytokine.q: What activates the classical complement pathway?
3_compliments_and_cytokine.l: Classical pathway activation requires antigen-antibody complexes.

3_compliments_and_cytokine.q: Which proteins make up C1?
3_compliments_and_cytokine.l: C1 consists of C1q, C1r, and C1s.

3_compliments_and_cytokine.q: Which component of C1 binds antibodies?
3_compliments_and_cytokine.l: C1q binds the Fc portion of IgG or IgM.

3_compliments_and_cytokine.q: C1q binds to which part of IgG or IgM?
3_compliments_and_cytokine.l: C1q binds the Fc portion of IgG or IgM.

3_compliments_and_cytokine.q: Activated C1s cleaves which complement proteins?
3_compliments_and_cytokine.l: Activated C1s cleaves C4 and C2.

3_compliments_and_cytokine.q: Which molecule regulates the classical complement pathway?
3_compliments_and_cytokine.l: C1 esterase inhibitor regulates the classical pathway.

3_compliments_and_cytokine.q: Deficiency of C1 esterase inhibitor causes which disease?
3_compliments_and_cytokine.l: C1 INH deficiency causes hereditary angioedema.

3_compliments_and_cytokine.q: Which complement components are decreased in hereditary angioedema?
3_compliments_and_cytokine.l: Hereditary angioedema is associated with low C4 and C2 levels.

3_compliments_and_cytokine.q: Excess production of which mediator causes hereditary angioedema?
3_compliments_and_cytokine.l: Hereditary angioedema results from excess bradykinin production.

3_compliments_and_cytokine.q: Which drugs are contraindicated in hereditary angioedema?
3_compliments_and_cytokine.l: ACE inhibitors increase bradykinin and worsen hereditary angioedema.

3_compliments_and_cytokine.q: Which complement pathway does not require antibodies?
3_compliments_and_cytokine.l: Both Alternative and Lectin pathways are antibody-independent.

3_compliments_and_cytokine.q: What activates the alternative pathway?
3_compliments_and_cytokine.l: The alternative pathway is activated by microbial PAMPs.

3_compliments_and_cytokine.q: Which complement component is spontaneously cleaved to initiate the alternative pathway?
3_compliments_and_cytokine.l: C3 is spontaneously cleaved to initiate the alternative pathway.

3_compliments_and_cytokine.q: Which factor binds to C3b in the alternative pathway?
3_compliments_and_cytokine.l: Factor B binds to C3b during alternative pathway activation.

3_compliments_and_cytokine.q: Factor D cleaves Factor B into which fragments?
3_compliments_and_cytokine.l: Factor D cleaves Factor B into Ba and Bb.

3_compliments_and_cytokine.q: What is the C3 convertase of the alternative pathway?
3_compliments_and_cytokine.l: C3bBb is the C3 convertase of the alternative pathway.

3_compliments_and_cytokine.q: Which molecule stabilizes C3bBb?
3_compliments_and_cytokine.l: Properdin is the positive regulator that stabilizes C3bBb.

3_compliments_and_cytokine.q: Which factors negatively regulate the alternative pathway?
3_compliments_and_cytokine.l: Factor H and Factor I are negative regulators of the alternative pathway.

3_compliments_and_cytokine.q: Factor I cleaves C3b into what product?
3_compliments_and_cytokine.l: Factor I cleaves C3b into iC3b.

3_compliments_and_cytokine.q: Which pathway is activated by Mannose Binding Lectin?
3_compliments_and_cytokine.l: The lectin pathway is activated by MBL.

3_compliments_and_cytokine.q: MBL binds which sugar on microbial surfaces?
3_compliments_and_cytokine.l: MBL binds mannose on bacterial and fungal surfaces.

3_compliments_and_cytokine.q: What enzyme is activated by MBL?
3_compliments_and_cytokine.l: MBL activates MASP in the lectin pathway.

3_compliments_and_cytokine.q: MASP cleaves which complement proteins?
3_compliments_and_cytokine.l: MASP cleaves C4 and C2 like the classical pathway.

3_compliments_and_cytokine.q: Does the lectin pathway require antibodies?
3_compliments_and_cytokine.l: The lectin pathway does not require antibodies.

4_antigen_antibody.q: What is a hapten?
4_antigen_antibody.l: Haptens are too small to induce an immune response alone.

4_antigen_antibody.q: A hapten becomes immunogenic when attached to a:
4_antigen_antibody.l: Carrier proteins convert haptens into immunogens.

4_antigen_antibody.q: Which toxin is a classic superantigen?
4_antigen_antibody.l: TSST-1 from S. aureus acts as a superantigen.

4_antigen_antibody.q: Superantigens bind directly to which MHC molecule?
4_antigen_antibody.l: Superantigens bind directly to MHC II without processing.

4_antigen_antibody.q: Superantigens bind the Vβ region of which receptor?
4_antigen_antibody.l: Superantigens cross-link MHC II and TCR Vβ.

4_antigen_antibody.q: The systemic toxicity of superantigens is due to massive release of:
4_antigen_antibody.l: Superantigens trigger a cytokine storm.

4_antigen_antibody.q: Which cytokine is massively released from T cells by superantigens?
4_antigen_antibody.l: IL-2 is a major cytokine released during superantigen activation.

4_antigen_antibody.q: What is an epitope?
4_antigen_antibody.l: An epitope is the specific part of an antigen recognized by receptors.

4_antigen_antibody.q: Which antigens are generally the best immunogens?
4_antigen_antibody.l: Complex proteins are highly immunogenic.

4_antigen_antibody.q: Proteins become strongly immunogenic when their size exceeds:
4_antigen_antibody.l: Proteins larger than 10 kDa are usually immunogenic.

4_antigen_antibody.q: An antibody molecule contains how many chains?
4_antigen_antibody.l: Antibodies contain 2 heavy and 2 light chains.

4_antigen_antibody.q: The antigen-binding site is formed by:
4_antigen_antibody.l: Variable regions of heavy and light chains form the binding site.

4_antigen_antibody.q: How many antigen-binding sites are present on one antibody monomer?
4_antigen_antibody.l: Each antibody monomer has two identical binding sites.

4_antigen_antibody.q: Which region of the antibody binds antigen?
4_antigen_antibody.l: Fab contains the antigen-binding site.

4_antigen_antibody.q: Which region determines antibody function and isotype?
4_antigen_antibody.l: Fc determines effector function and antibody class.

4_antigen_antibody.q: Which part of the antibody provides flexibility?
4_antigen_antibody.l: The hinge region allows movement of Fab arms.

4_antigen_antibody.q: Which immunoglobulin classes have four heavy-chain constant domains?
4_antigen_antibody.l: IgM and IgE contain four CH domains.

4_antigen_antibody.q: Heavy-chain genes are located on which chromosome?
4_antigen_antibody.l: All heavy-chain genes are on chromosome 14.

4_antigen_antibody.q: Kappa light-chain genes are located on chromosome:
4_antigen_antibody.l: Kappa light-chain genes are found on chromosome 2.

4_antigen_antibody.q: Lambda light-chain genes are located on chromosome:
4_antigen_antibody.l: Lambda light-chain genes are found on chromosome 22.

4_antigen_antibody.q: A single antibody contains:
a: Either κ or λ chains only
4_antigen_antibody.l: An antibody contains either kappa or lambda light chains, never both.

4_antigen_antibody.q: Bence Jones proteins are:
4_antigen_antibody.l: Bence Jones proteins are free κ or λ light chains.

4_antigen_antibody.q: Idiotype refers to:
4_antigen_antibody.l: Idiotype is determined by the antigen-binding region.

4_antigen_antibody.q: How many CDRs contribute to one antigen-binding site?
4_antigen_antibody.l: Three CDRs from VH and three from VL form one site.

4_antigen_antibody.q: Allotypes are useful in:
4_antigen_antibody.l: Allotypes reflect inherited Fc differences and aid paternity testing.

4_antigen_antibody.q: Which antibody is the most abundant in serum?
4_antigen_antibody.l: IgG accounts for about 80% of serum immunoglobulins.

4_antigen_antibody.q: Which antibody crosses the placenta?
4_antigen_antibody.l: IgG is the only immunoglobulin that crosses the placenta.

4_antigen_antibody.q: Which antibody is the best activator of complement?
4_antigen_antibody.l: IgM is the most efficient activator of complement.

4_antigen_antibody.q: Which antibody is produced first during a primary immune response?
4_antigen_antibody.l: IgM is the first antibody produced after antigen exposure.

4_antigen_antibody.q: Which antibody protects mucosal surfaces?
4_antigen_antibody.l: IgA is the principal mucosal antibody.

4_antigen_antibody.q: Secretory IgA exists as a:
4_antigen_antibody.l: Secretory IgA is a dimer linked by a J chain.

4_antigen_antibody.q: Which antibody mediates allergic reactions?
4_antigen_antibody.l: IgE binds mast cells and mediates Type I hypersensitivity.

4_antigen_antibody.q: Which antibody is important against helminths?
4_antigen_antibody.l: IgE and eosinophils mediate defense against parasites.

4_antigen_antibody.q: Which antibody functions as a B-cell receptor on naïve B cells?
4_antigen_antibody.l: IgD is found primarily on naïve B cells.

4_antigen_antibody.q: The first antibody synthesized by a fetus is:
4_antigen_antibody.l: IgM is the first immunoglobulin produced by the fetus.

4_antigen_antibody.q: Elevated fetal IgM suggests:
4_antigen_antibody.l: Fetal IgM indicates in utero infection because IgM cannot cross the placenta.

5_antigen_receptors.q: A mature naïve B cell expresses which immunoglobulins on its surface?
5_antigen_receptors.l: Mature naïve B cells express membrane-bound IgM and IgD.

5_antigen_receptors.q: Which receptor binds unprocessed antigens directly?
5_antigen_receptors.l: BCR recognizes native unprocessed antigens.

5_antigen_receptors.q: T-cell receptors recognize:
5_antigen_receptors.l: TCR recognizes processed peptide antigens presented on MHC.

5_antigen_receptors.q: Which molecule mediates signal transduction in T cells?
5_antigen_receptors.l: CD3 transmits activation signals into T cells.

5_antigen_receptors.q: Which molecules mediate signal transduction in B cells?
5_antigen_receptors.l: Igα and Igβ form the B-cell signaling complex.

5_antigen_receptors.q: CD21 (CR2) serves as the receptor for:
5_antigen_receptors.l: EBV infects B cells through CD21.

5_antigen_receptors.q: The B-cell co-receptor complex consists of:
5_antigen_receptors.l: CD19, CD21, and CD81 form the B-cell co-receptor complex.

5_antigen_receptors.q: Approximately 95% of circulating T cells express:
5_antigen_receptors.l: Most circulating T cells express αβ TCR.

5_antigen_receptors.q: γδ T cells are primarily involved in:
5_antigen_receptors.l: γδ T cells contribute to innate and mucosal immunity.

5_antigen_receptors.q: Which co-receptors participate in TCR signaling?
5_antigen_receptors.l: CD4 and CD8 bind MHC molecules during T-cell activation.

5_antigen_receptors.q: Heavy-chain variable regions are formed by:
5_antigen_receptors.l: Heavy chains undergo VDJ recombination.

5_antigen_receptors.q: Light-chain variable regions are formed by:
5_antigen_receptors.l: Light chains undergo VJ recombination only.

5_antigen_receptors.q: The β chain of TCR undergoes:
5_antigen_receptors.l: TCR β chain forms by VDJ recombination.

5_antigen_receptors.q: The α chain of TCR undergoes:
5_antigen_receptors.l: TCR α chain forms by VJ recombination.

5_antigen_receptors.q: Which genes encode the recombinase enzymes?
5_antigen_receptors.l: RAG1 and RAG2 are essential for VDJ recombination.

5_antigen_receptors.q: Omenn syndrome is caused by:
5_antigen_receptors.l: Omenn syndrome results from partial RAG activity.

5_antigen_receptors.q: A nonsense mutation in RAG1/RAG2 may cause:
5_antigen_receptors.l: Complete loss of recombination can cause SCID.

5_antigen_receptors.q: Bruton agammaglobulinemia results from deficiency of:
5_antigen_receptors.l: BTK deficiency arrests B-cell maturation.

5_antigen_receptors.q: B-cell development is arrested at which stage in Bruton agammaglobulinemia?
5_antigen_receptors.l: Bruton disease causes arrest at the pre-B-cell stage.

5_antigen_receptors.q: Which enzyme generates junctional diversity?
5_antigen_receptors.l: TdT randomly inserts nucleotides at gene junctions.

5_antigen_receptors.q: TdT acts mainly during:
5_antigen_receptors.l: TdT is active during heavy-chain recombination.

5_antigen_receptors.q: TdT is turned off during:
5_antigen_receptors.l: TdT is inactive during light-chain rearrangement.

5_antigen_receptors.q: TdT is a marker for:
5_antigen_receptors.l: TdT is expressed in lymphoblasts of ALL.

5_antigen_receptors.q: Finding TdT-positive lymphocytes in blood suggests:
5_antigen_receptors.l: Circulating TdT-positive cells suggest ALL.

5_antigen_receptors.q: Which immunoglobulin is expressed on immature B cells?
5_antigen_receptors.l: Immature B cells express surface IgM only.

5_antigen_receptors.q: Which immunoglobulins are expressed on mature naïve B cells?
5_antigen_receptors.l: Mature B cells express both IgM and IgD.

5_antigen_receptors.q: Class switching is:
5_antigen_receptors.l: Once a B cell switches class, it cannot switch back.

5_antigen_receptors.q: The first immunoglobulin expressed during B-cell development is:
5_antigen_receptors.l: IgM is the first antibody expressed by developing B cells.

5_antigen_receptors.q: RAG expression occurs in which stages?
5_antigen_receptors.l: RAG is expressed during early B-cell development.

5_antigen_receptors.q: Allelic exclusion ensures:
a: One antibody specificity per B cell
5_antigen_receptors.l: Allelic exclusion ensures identical idiotypes on a B cell.

5_antigen_receptors.q: The pre-B-cell marker is:
5_antigen_receptors.l: Pre-B cells contain cytoplasmic μ heavy chains.

5_antigen_receptors.q: The immature B-cell marker is:
5_antigen_receptors.l: Immature B cells express surface IgM.

5_antigen_receptors.q: The mature B-cell marker is:
5_antigen_receptors.l: Mature naïve B cells express IgM and IgD.

5_antigen_receptors.q: Lymphocytes lacking antigen receptors are:
5_antigen_receptors.l: Antigen receptors are essential for lymphocyte function.

5_antigen_receptors.q: Which process contributes most to antibody diversity?
5_antigen_receptors.l: TdT-generated junctional diversity exceeds VDJ recombination alone.

5_antigen_receptors.q: Which immunoglobulin classes can be produced after class switching?
5_antigen_receptors.l: Class switching allows production of IgG, IgA, and IgE.

5_antigen_receptors.q: Bone marrow transplantation is a treatment for:
5_antigen_receptors.l: Omenn syndrome may be treated with bone marrow transplantation.

5_antigen_receptors.q: γδ T cells are found mainly in:
5_antigen_receptors.l: γδ T cells are enriched in blood and mucosal tissues.

5_antigen_receptors.q: CD3 is functionally equivalent to which B-cell molecules?
5_antigen_receptors.l: CD3 and Igα/Igβ are signal transduction complexes.

5_antigen_receptors.q: Failure of CD3 expression results in:
a: Failure of signaling after antigen recognition
5_antigen_receptors.l: Antigen recognition occurs, but signaling cannot proceed.

6_humoral_immune_response.q: Humoral immunity is primarily responsible for controlling:
6_humoral_immune_response.l: Humoral immunity controls extracellular pathogens using antibodies.
B cells are responsible for antibody-mediated immunity.
T cells mediate cellular immunity.
BCRs bind native unprocessed antigens directly.

6_humoral_immune_response.q: Humoral immune responses begin mainly in:
6_humoral_immune_response.l: Humoral responses are initiated in secondary lymphoid organs.
Mature naive B cells express both IgM and IgD.
Activated B cells proliferate by clonal expansion.

6_humoral_immune_response.q: The antibody-secreting form of B cells is:
6_humoral_immune_response.l: Plasma cells produce antibodies.
T-dependent responses require helper T-cell assistance.
T-independent antigens commonly lack protein components.

6_humoral_immune_response.q: T-independent antigens activate B cells by:
6_humoral_immune_response.l: Large multivalent antigens cross-link many BCRs.

6_humoral_immune_response.q: The main antibody produced in T-independent responses is:
6_humoral_immune_response.l: T-independent responses produce mainly IgM.
T-independent antigens generate little or no memory.
PPSV23 contains unconjugated polysaccharide antigen.
PCV13 is polysaccharide conjugated to diphtheria toxoid.
Follicular B cells are the major B-cell population.

6_humoral_immune_response.q: B cells present antigen to Tfh cells using:
6_humoral_immune_response.l: B cells present processed antigen on MHC II.

6_humoral_immune_response.q: A critical interaction required for class switching is:
6_humoral_immune_response.l: Class switching requires CD40 on B cells and CD40L on T cells.

6_humoral_immune_response.q: The first wave of activated B cells mainly produces:
6_humoral_immune_response.l: Primary antibody response is predominantly IgM.

6_humoral_immune_response.q: The second wave of activated B cells occurs in:
6_humoral_immune_response.l: Affinity maturation and class switching occur in germinal centers.
Class switching occurs within germinal centers.
Somatic hypermutation occurs in germinal centers.
Affinity measures the strength of a single interaction.
Avidity is the overall strength of all antibody-antigen interactions.

6_humoral_immune_response.q: Which immunoglobulin has the highest avidity?
6_humoral_immune_response.l: Pentameric IgM has the highest avidity.
Somatic hypermutation affects variable region genes.
Affinity maturation increases antigen-binding strength.
Class switching changes the heavy-chain constant region.
The variable region remains unchanged.

6_humoral_immune_response.q: A B cell can switch from IgM to:
6_humoral_immune_response.l: IgM can switch to IgG, IgA, or IgE.
Deleted DNA cannot be restored.
IL-4 drives class switching to IgE.
IL-13 assists IgE class switching.
IL-5 enhances switching to IgA.

6_humoral_immune_response.q: Hyper-IgM syndrome is caused by defective:
6_humoral_immune_response.l: Failure of CD40-CD40L interaction prevents class switching.

6_humoral_immune_response.q: Patients with Hyper-IgM syndrome cannot produce:
6_humoral_immune_response.l: Class switching fails, leaving only IgM production.

6_humoral_immune_response.q: The predominant immunoglobulin in Hyper-IgM syndrome is:
6_humoral_immune_response.l: Only IgM is produced.
Recurrent respiratory infections are characteristic.

6_humoral_immune_response.q: Hyper-IgM syndrome may also cause recurrent:
6_humoral_immune_response.l: GI infections are common due to impaired mucosal immunity.

6_humoral_immune_response.q: Memory B cells are generated mainly during:
6_humoral_immune_response.l: Memory B cells arise during germinal center reactions.

7_mhc.q: The HLA genes that encode MHC proteins are located on:
7_mhc.l: HLA genes are clustered on chromosome 6.

7_mhc.q: MHC proteins are best described as:
7_mhc.l: MHC proteins differ among members of the same species and are alloantigens.

7_mhc.q: MHC Class III locus encodes:
7_mhc.l: Class III encodes complement components and cytokines, not histocompatibility antigens.

7_mhc.q: MHC Class I consists of:
a: Alpha and beta chains of equal size
7_mhc.l: Class I contains an α heavy chain associated with β2-microglobulin.

7_mhc.q: β2-microglobulin is required for:
7_mhc.l: Without β2-microglobulin, MHC I cannot be expressed.

7_mhc.q: A mutation in β2-microglobulin results in:
7_mhc.l: β2-microglobulin mutations prevent MHC I surface expression.

7_mhc.q: The peptide-binding groove of MHC I typically holds:
7_mhc.l: MHC I binds short peptides of 8-9 amino acids.

7_mhc.q: The peptide-binding groove of MHC II typically holds:
7_mhc.l: MHC II accommodates longer peptides of 11+ amino acids.

7_mhc.q: MHC Class I molecules bind to:
7_mhc.l: MHC I presents antigens to CD8+ cytotoxic T cells.

7_mhc.q: MHC Class II molecules bind to:
7_mhc.l: MHC II presents antigens to CD4+ helper T cells.

7_mhc.q: MHC Class I loci include:
7_mhc.l: Class I molecules are encoded by HLA-A, HLA-B, and HLA-C.

7_mhc.q: MHC Class II loci include:
7_mhc.l: Class II molecules are encoded by HLA-DP, DQ, and DR.

7_mhc.q: MHC Class I is expressed on:
7_mhc.l: All nucleated cells express MHC I.

7_mhc.q: Which cell does NOT express MHC Class I?
7_mhc.l: Red blood cells lack nuclei and do not express MHC I.

7_mhc.q: MHC Class II is primarily expressed on:
7_mhc.l: Class II is expressed on professional antigen-presenting cells.

7_mhc.q: MHC Class I presents which type of antigen?
7_mhc.l: Class I presents endogenous antigens such as viral proteins.

7_mhc.q: MHC Class II presents which type of antigen?
7_mhc.l: Class II presents extracellular antigens to helper T cells.

7_mhc.q: Endogenous antigens are degraded in:
7_mhc.l: Viral and cytosolic proteins are processed by proteasomes.

7_mhc.q: TAP transports peptides into the:
7_mhc.l: TAP delivers peptides into the ER for MHC I loading.

7_mhc.q: TAP deficiency causes deficiency of:
7_mhc.l: TAP deficiency impairs MHC I loading and reduces CD8+ T cells.

7_mhc.q: Extracellular proteins are processed in:
7_mhc.l: Class II antigens are processed in endosomes and lysosomes.

7_mhc.q: The invariant chain functions to:
a: Block the MHC II peptide groove
7_mhc.l: Invariant chain prevents premature peptide loading onto MHC II.

7_mhc.q: Invariant chain is removed in:
7_mhc.l: Acidic vesicles dissolve the invariant chain to allow peptide loading.

7_mhc.q: MHC molecules without peptide are:
7_mhc.l: MHC molecules require peptide loading for stability.

7_mhc.q: MHC molecules without peptide will:
7_mhc.l: Peptide loading is required for surface expression.

7_mhc.q: MHC Class II deficiency results in deficiency of:
7_mhc.l: MHC II deficiency impairs CD4+ T-cell development.

7_mhc.q: Expression of MHC alleles is:
7_mhc.l: Both maternal and paternal MHC alleles are expressed equally.

7_mhc.q: The combination of MHC alleles on one chromosome is called a:
7_mhc.l: A haplotype refers to the MHC genes inherited together on one chromosome.

7_mhc.q: The chance that siblings are an identical HLA match is:
7_mhc.l: Siblings have a 25% chance of sharing both haplotypes.

7_mhc.q: A parent used as a transplant donor is considered a:
7_mhc.l: Parents always share one haplotype with their children.

8_cell_mediated_immune_response.q: A 5-year-old boy presents to his pediatrician with recurrent sinopulmonary infections. Lab testing reveals normal numbers of T and B lymphocytes, but further evaluation demonstrates that his antigen-presenting cells are unable to stabilize firm interactions with T cells during antigen presentation. A genetic mutation disrupting which of the following cell surface molecules is most likely responsible for this child's condition?
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.l: Firm adhesion between T cells and antigen-presenting cells (APCs) is mediated by accessory molecules, notably the interaction between LFA-1 on the T cell and ICAM-1 on the APC.
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.h: Think about the adhesion pairs responsible for strengthening the binding between a T cell and an APC.

8_cell_mediated_immune_response.q: A 22-year-old medical student is investigating the mechanisms of T cell activation in a laboratory experiment. She observes that when naive helper T cells encounter a specific viral antigen displayed on dendritic cells, they rapidly produce a particular cytokine that acts in an autocrine fashion to drive their own clonal expansion. Binding of this cytokine to which of the following receptors is directly responsible for this proliferative response?
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.l: Full activation of T cells leads to the secretion of IL-2 and the expression of high-affinity IL-2 receptors, which binds IL-2 to stimulate autocrine cell proliferation and clonal expansion.
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.h: Identify the primary cytokine and its matching receptor that are responsible for the proliferation of activated T-cell clones.

8_cell_mediated_immune_response.q: An investigator studies T lymphocyte regulation by analyzing cell surface receptor interactions during prolonged exposure to antigens. She notes that a specific receptor expressed on activated T cells binds to B7-1 and B7-2 on antigen-presenting cells to deliver an inhibitory signal, effectively terminating the proliferative response and preventing autoimmunity. Which of the following molecules is this receptor?
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.l: CTLA-4 is an inhibitory receptor induced on activated T cells that competes with CD28 to bind B7-1/B7-2 on APCs, delivering an inhibitory signal that blocks IL-2 production and halts clonal expansion.
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.h: Consider the inhibitory receptor that shares the exact same B7 ligands as the costimulatory receptor CD28.

8_cell_mediated_immune_response.q: A 34-year-old female with a history of persistent viral infections is found to have a genetic defect that impairs the function of her CD8+ Cytotoxic T Lymphocytes (CTLs). Biopsy of an infected tissue shows that her CTLs can successfully attach to virus-infected host cells via MHC Class I complexes, but they are completely unable to induce target cell apoptosis. A deficiency in which of the following pairs of granular components most likely explains these findings?
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.l: CD8+ cytotoxic T lymphocytes kill infected cells via exocytosis of granule contents, specifically perforin, which forms polyperforin membrane channels, and granzymes, which enter the target cell to activate caspases.
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.h: Recall the two major proteins stored in CTL granules that punch holes in membranes and activate the caspase cascade.

8_cell_mediated_immune_response.q: A 45-year-old male with severe Crohn's disease is treated with a monoclonal antibody that targets a specific cytokine pathway. This therapeutic intervention effectively reduces the recruitment and activation of neutrophils to mucosal surfaces, thereby diminishing chronic extracellular inflammation. The targeted pathway most likely disrupts the differentiation or function of which T helper cell subset?
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.l: Th17 cells secrete IL-17, IL-21, and IL-22, which function to recruit neutrophils to fight extracellular microbes and drive inflammatory reactions.
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.h: Identify the specific CD4+ T helper cell subset whose primary role is defense against extracellular bacteria and fungi via neutrophil recruitment.

8_cell_mediated_immune_response.q: A researcher is studying the tissue-homing properties of memory T lymphocytes. She isolates a group of memory T cells from a patient's peripheral tissues that lack lymphoid-homing receptors such as CCR7 and L-selectin (CD62L). These specific memory cells demonstrate an ability to produce immediate effector cytokines locally upon antigen re-challenge. Which of the following best classifies these cells?
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.l: Effector memory T cells preferentially reside in peripheral tissues, lack CCR7 and CD62L, and provide immediate local protection through the rapid production of effector cytokines.
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.h: Differentiate between memory cells that home to secondary lymphoid organs and those that reside directly in peripheral tissues.

8_cell_mediated_immune_response.q: A 29-year-old male is diagnosed with a severe parasitic worm infection (helminthiasis). Laboratory studies show a marked elevation in peripheral blood eosinophils and serum IgE levels. The differentiation of the T helper subset responsible for driving this specific protective response is orchestrated by which of the following master transcription factors?
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.l: Th2 cells express the master transcription factor GATA3 and secrete IL-4, IL-5, and IL-13, which are essential for driving IgE production, eosinophil recruitment, and defense against helminths.
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.h: Think of the master transcription factor associated with the Th2 lineage, which regulates humoral and anti-parasitic immunity.

8_cell_mediated_immune_response.q: An experimental model evaluates the host response to Mycobacterium tuberculosis infection. The investigators observe that the absolute clearance of these facultative intracellular bacteria relies heavily on a cytokine secreted by Th1 cells that functions to classically activate macrophages, boosting their microbicidal activity. Which cytokine is primarily responsible for this macrophage activation?
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.l: Th1 cells produce IFN-gamma, which is the principal macrophage-activating cytokine that stimulates M1 macrophages to generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) and nitric oxide (NO) to kill phagocytosed microbes.
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.h: Identify the signature pro-inflammatory cytokine produced by Th1 cells that drives classical macrophage (M1) activation.

8_cell_mediated_immune_response.q: A newborn infant is found to have a rare genetic syndrome presenting with severe, systemic autoimmunity due to a complete failure in peripheral immune tolerance. Genetic sequencing confirms a loss-of-function mutation in the gene encoding the FoxP3 transcription factor. Which of the following cell types is directly affected by this mutation?
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.l: Regulatory T cells (Tregs) uniquely express the master transcription factor FoxP3 and CD25, and function to prevent autoimmunity by maintaining tolerance to self-antigens.
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.h: Identify the T cell population responsible for suppressing self-reactive T cells and maintaining self-tolerance.

8_cell_mediated_immune_response.q: An analysis of an immune response against a viral pathogen demonstrates that some activated CD4+ T cells do not enter the systemic circulation to home to the site of infection. Instead, these cells remain within the secondary lymphoid organ and migrate directly into the lymphoid follicles. What is the primary functional role of this specific T cell population?
a: Helping B cells to produce high-affinity antibodies
Recruiting neutrophils to the area of tissue injury
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.l: Some activated CD4+ T cells remain within the lymph node, migrate into B cell follicles, and function to help B cells proliferate and differentiate into antibody-producing cells.
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.h: Consider what happens inside the lymphoid follicles and which cell type requires T-cell help to refine its defense strategy.

8_cell_mediated_immune_response.q: A 31-year-old scientist is studying how different pathogens skew the immune system. She notes that when dendritic cells encounter specific intracellular pathogens, they produce high levels of Interleukin-12 (IL-12). The presence of IL-12 during the initial activation of naive T cells ($Th0$) will directly promote differentiation into which of the following lineages?
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.l: Microbes that stimulate the innate immune system to produce IL-12 and IFN-gamma polarize naive CD4+ T cells ($Th0$) to differentiate into the Th1 effector subset.
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.h: Determine which helper subset is induced by pro-inflammatory signals like IL-12 to manage intracellular infections.

8_cell_mediated_immune_response.q: A 45-year-old male with an advanced stage of Human Immunodeficiency Virus (HIV) infection presents with a low CD4+ T cell count. He is determined to be at a critically high risk for the reactivation of past latent intracellular infections, such as Mycobacterium tuberculosis and Herpes Simplex Virus. This vulnerability is primarily caused by a failure in which branch of the immune system?
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.l: Cell-mediated immunity (CMI), driven by CD4+ and CD8+ T cells, is specifically responsible for defending against facultative and obligate intracellular pathogens.
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.h: Think of the branch of adaptive immunity that handles microbes hiding inside host vacuoles or the cytoplasm.

8_cell_mediated_immune_response.q: A 19-year-old female presents with symptoms of an acute viral upper respiratory infection. At the cellular level, viral cytosolic proteins are being degraded within her infected airway epithelial cells, and the resulting viral peptides are being processed for presentation. Which of the following molecules must these viral peptides associate with on the cell surface to be recognized by her adaptive cytotoxic effector cells?
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.l: Antigens derived from proteins in the cytosol (such as viral proteins) are processed and displayed on MHC Class I molecules, which are recognized by CD8+ cytotoxic T cells.
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.h: Recall which MHC class displays intracellular or cytosolic antigens to cytotoxic T cells.

8_cell_mediated_immune_response.q: A laboratory technician accidentally punctures his finger with a needle containing a suspension of Listeria monocytogenes. Dendritic cells in the local epithelium capture the bacterial antigens and begin migrating to the regional lymph nodes. During this migration process, which of the following phenotypic changes will these dendritic cells undergo to ensure optimal antigen presentation?
a: Upregulation of MHC and costimulatory molecules
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.l: As dendritic cells capture antigen and migrate to lymphoid organs, they mature, upregulating their expression of MHC molecules and costimulators (like B7) to activate naive T cells.
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.h: Consider the structural changes an APC needs to fully activate a naive T cell.

8_cell_mediated_immune_response.q: A 62-year-old female is undergoing immunotherapy for a malignant tumor. The oncologists utilize a drug that blocks the interaction between the T-cell receptor PD-1 and its ligands, PD-L1/PD-L2, on the tumor cells. This checkpoint inhibition prevents tumor cells from using these molecules to induce which of the following cellular states in tumor-specific T cells?
a: Functional inhibition and termination of response
Upregulation of homing receptors to the lymph node
Enhanced production of perforin and granzymes
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.l: PD-1 is an inhibitory receptor expressed on activated T cells; binding to its ligands delivers inhibitory signals that terminate T cell responses and limit tissue damage.
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.h: Think about the normal, native function of coinhibitory pathways like PD-1/PD-L1 when they are activated.

8_cell_mediated_immune_response.q: An in vitro study of human T cell activation shows that when a naive CD4+ T cell binds an antigen-MHC Class II complex via its TCR, it requires a simultaneous second signal from the antigen-presenting cell to achieve full functional activation. Which of the following pairs of molecules represents the classic costimulatory match required for this second signal?
a: CD28 binding to B7-1 or B7-2
CD4 binding to the MHC Class II beta-2 domain
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.l: The primary second signal required for full naive T cell activation is costimulation, achieved when the CD28 receptor on the T cell binds to CD80 (B7-1) or CD86 (B7-2) on the APC.
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.h: Look for the classic costimulatory receptor on naive T cells and its associated ligand on professional APCs.

8_cell_mediated_immune_response.q: A 23-year-old male with an acute intracellular bacterial infection exhibits a robust immune response. Effector CD4+ T cells of the Th1 subset migrate to the site of infection and release cytokines. These cytokines promote a classical macrophage activation pathway (M1), leading to the destruction of the ingested microbes. Which set of microbicidal substances is primarily produced by these M1 macrophages to achieve this killing?
a: Reactive oxygen species (ROS), nitric oxide (NO), and lysosomal enzymes
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.l: Classically activated macrophages (M1) are induced by PAMPs and IFN-gamma to produce ROS, NO, and lysosomal enzymes, which perform microbicidal actions to kill ingested bacteria and fungi.
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.h: Identify the highly toxic chemical radicals and enzymes used by M1 macrophages to destroy phagocytosed pathogens.

8_cell_mediated_immune_response.q: A patient with a chronic inflammatory condition demonstrates a highly activated immune profile. On a molecular level, helper T cells are interacting with antigen-presenting cells via cell-surface molecules. During this process, CD40L (CD154) on the activated T cell binds to CD40 on the APC. Which of the following best describes the immediate physiological consequence of this CD40-CD40L interaction?
a: Immediate apoptosis of the helper T cell
Activation of the APC to express more B7 molecules and secrete activating cytokines
Inhibition of MHC Class I expression on the target cell
Downregulation of adhesion pairs like LFA-1 and ICAM-1
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.l: The binding of CD40L on activated T cells to CD40 on APCs activates the APCs, prompting them to express more B7 costimulators and secrete cytokines that enhance T cell differentiation.
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.h: Consider how an activated T cell sends feedback to an APC to boost its costimulatory capacity.

8_cell_mediated_immune_response.q: An experimental cell line consists of mutant naive CD4+ T cells that lack the expression of the CD3 surface molecule. When these mutant T cells are cultured with professional antigen-presenting cells displaying highly specific, high-affinity peptide-MHC complexes, no proliferative or downstream activation response occurs. Which of the following functions is missing in these cells?
a: Binding to the nonpolymorphic region of MHC Class II
Transduction of the signal indicating specific antigen binding has occurred
Direct synthesis of the Interleukin-2 cytokine molecule
Upregulation of adhesion pairs like CD2 and CD58
Binding to B7-1 and B7-2 costimulators
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.l: The CD3 complex is non-covalently associated with the TCR and functions to transduce the biochemical signals into the T cell nucleus once specific antigen binding occurs.
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.h: Identify the role of the CD3 complex in the T-cell receptor apparatus.

8_cell_mediated_immune_response.q: A research group investigates the host response against extracellular fungal infections of the skin. They find that defense against these organisms relies heavily on a specialized helper T cell subset. This subset is uniquely induced by a combination of IL-6 and TGF-beta, and it expresses the master transcription factor RORgammat. Which of the following cell surface chemokine receptors is characteristically expressed by this effector subset?
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.l: Th17 cells are induced by IL-6 and TGF-beta, express the transcription factor RORgammat, and express the chemokine receptor CCR6 to guide them to sites of extracellular infection.
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.h: Recall the unique chemokine receptor associated with the Th17 helper T cell lineage.

8_cell_mediated_immune_response.q: A 35-year-old woman is evaluated for a suspected localized tissue reaction. Histological analysis reveals an abundance of alternatively activated macrophages (M2) within the affected area. Which of the following pairs of cytokines is primarily responsible for driving monocytes down this alternative M2 differentiation pathway?
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.l: Alternatively activated macrophages (M2) are induced by the cytokines Interleukin-4 (IL-4) and Interleukin-13 (IL-13) and function mainly in tissue repair and anti-inflammatory pathways.
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.h: Look for the cytokines produced by Th2 cells that act as a signal for alternative macrophage activation.

8_cell_mediated_immune_response.q: An immunologist isolates cell populations from a human lymph node. He identifies a group of T lymphocytes that express CD3 and CD4, but lack CCR7 and L-selectin expression. These cells are found to home preferentially to peripheral tissues rather than secondary lymphoid organs, providing rapid local cytokine production upon antigen challenge. These cells are best classified as which of the following?
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.l: Effector memory T cells preferentially localize to peripheral tissues, lack lymphoid-homing receptors (CCR7, CD62L), and provide immediate local protection.
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.h: Focus on the subtype of memory T cells that guards peripheral tissues and lacks lymphoid-homing markers.

8_cell_mediated_immune_response.q: A laboratory scientist studies the clearance of intracellular bacteria using an in vitro model. She observes that an infected macrophage is under severe metabolic stress because the intracellular pathogen is actively blocking the fusion of the phagosome with the lysosome. In a normal host, this macrophage will secrete a specific cytokine to signal naive helper T cells, leading to downstream production of IFN-gamma. Which cytokine does the stressed macrophage release to initiate this specific loop?
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.l: When intracellular microbes resist phagocytic destruction, macrophages secrete IL-12, which binds to receptors on CD4+ T cells to induce differentiation and release of IFN-gamma.
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.h: Identify the cytokine secreted by macrophages to induce a Th1 response during intracellular infection.

8_cell_mediated_immune_response.q: A 53-year-old male presenting with a chronic cough and weight loss is found to have a positive tuberculin skin test. A biopsy of a pulmonary nodule reveals extensive granulomatous inflammation containing classically activated macrophages (M1). Which of the following markers or secretor products would most characteristically confirm that these macrophages have been classically activated?
a: High levels of IL-10 and TGF-beta
Upregulated production of ROS, nitric oxide, and IL-12
Secretions of growth factors for wound healing
Upregulation of the FoxP3 transcription factor
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.l: Classically activated macrophages (M1) are induced by PAMPs and IFN-gamma to secrete pro-inflammatory cytokines like IL-1, IL-12, and IL-23, and produce microbicidal ROS and NO.
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.h: Consider the key pro-inflammatory cytokines and microbicidal molecules characteristically produced by M1 macrophages.

8_cell_mediated_immune_response.q: A medical student is constructing a summary table of helper T cell cross-regulation for his upcoming immunology exam. He wants to highlight the mechanism by which one helper T cell pathway can directly suppress another. Which of the following cytokines, secreted by the Th2 subset, acts to directly inhibit the differentiation and action of the pro-inflammatory Th1 subset?
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.l: The Th2 subset secretes IL-4 and IL-10, which act to cross-regulate and inhibit the development and effector actions of the Th1 subset.
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.h: Identify the primary signature cytokine of the Th2 subset that suppresses Th1 development.

8_cell_mediated_immune_response.q: A 24-year-old female presents to the emergency department after being stung by a wasp. Within minutes, she develops diffuse urticaria and respiratory wheezing. This type I hypersensitivity reaction is initiated by IgE antibodies. Which T helper cell subset is directly responsible for promoting the class-switching of B cells to produce IgE?
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.l: Th2 cells secrete cytokines like IL-4, which drive B cell immunoglobulin class-switching to IgE, a key component in parasite defense and type I hypersensitivity reactions.
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.h: Recall the CD4+ T cell subset that produces IL-4 and handles humoral immunity and allergic responses.

8_cell_mediated_immune_response.q: An investigator is analyzing the cell surface phenotypes of various human lymphocytes. He isolates a unique population of cytotoxic cells that express the surface markers CD16 and CD56. These cells are found to participate in the innate immune response, kill virus-infected host cells without prior antigen exposure, and do not generate immunologic memory. Which of the following cells has the investigator isolated?
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.l: Natural Killer (NK) cells are innate lymphocytes characterized by the surface expression of CD16 and CD56; they kill infected cells without prior exposure and do not generate memory.
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.h: Look for the classic innate cytotoxic lymphocyte markers mentioned in the scenario.

8_cell_mediated_immune_response.q: A pathological study evaluates tissue samples from a patient with a severe parasitic infection caused by an intestinal helminth. The tissue demonstrates a pronounced infiltration of eosinophils actively engaged in destroying the parasite. Which of the following cytokines, specifically secreted by Th2 effector cells, is directly responsible for the recruitment and activation of these eosinophils?
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.l: Th2 effector cells secrete Interleukin-5 (IL-5), which specifically functions to activate and recruit eosinophils to sites of helminthic infections.
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.h: Identify the specific helper T-cell cytokine that is classically linked to eosinophil growth and activation.

8_cell_mediated_immune_response.q: A clinical research team investigates a patient presenting with an unusual susceptibility to certain tumors and intracellular viral infections. Detailed analysis reveals that the patient's CD8+ T cells are structurally normal but fail to kill target cells. However, further testing shows that the patient's immune system can still destroy antibody-coated target cells using an alternative mechanism where NK cells and macrophages bind to the Fc region of the bound antibodies. What is this alternative cytotoxic mechanism called?
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.l: Antibody-Dependent Cell-Mediated Cytotoxicity (ADCC) is a process where effector cells (like NK cells, macrophages, or eosinophils) bind via their Fc receptors to antibodies bound to a target cell, triggering target lysis.
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.h: Identify the process where immune cells destroy an active target cell that has been pre-coated with IgG or IgE antibodies.

8_cell_mediated_immune_response.q: A 28-year-old male is participating in an immunology trial. He is exposed to a novel viral antigen. Two weeks later, investigators isolate his memory T lymphocytes from his secondary lymphoid organs. They discover that these cells express the surface markers CCR7 and L-selectin (CD62L). Even though these cells lack immediate effector functions, they proliferate rapidly and differentiate into active effector cells upon re-encountering the antigen. Which cell type is this?
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.l: Central memory T cells preferentially reside in secondary lymphoid organs, express CCR7 and CD62L, and mount powerful recall responses by rapidly proliferating and differentiating into effectors.
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.h: Focus on the specific class of memory T cells that returns to the secondary lymphoid organs to wait for a recall response.

8_cell_mediated_immune_response.q: A pediatric patient presents with a severe skin infection characterized by multiple pustules and abscesses. Genetic testing reveals a complete deletion of the master transcription factor RORgammat. Due to this mutation, the child's immune system fails to recruit neutrophils to the site of extracellular microbial invasion. This presentation is most consistent with a total deficiency in which of the following cell types?
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.l: Th17 cell differentiation requires the master transcription factor RORgammat. These cells function to recruit neutrophils to defend against extracellular bacteria and fungi.
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.h: Connect the master transcription factor RORgammat directly to its corresponding helper T-cell subset.

8_cell_mediated_immune_response.q: An experimental model evaluates the functional differences between branches of cell-mediated cytotoxicity. Investigators introduce a viral pathogen into a culture of host cells. Some of these virus-infected cells attempt to evade immune detection by completely downregulating their surface expression of MHC Class I molecules. Which of the following immune cells will recognize this 'missing self' phenotype and execute targeted lysis of these evasive cells?
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.l: NK cells use inhibitory receptors that recognize self MHC Class I. If a virus or tumor downregulates MHC Class I expression ('missing self'), the inhibitory signal is lost, activating the NK cell to destroy the target cell.
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.h: Identify the innate cytotoxic cell type that kills host cells when it notices a complete lack or downregulation of MHC Class I.

8_cell_mediated_immune_response.q: A 30-year-old medical resident is reviewing the life cycle of adaptive immunity. He notes that after naive T lymphocytes recognize their specific antigens displayed on dendritic cells within the lymph node paracortex, they undergo extensive clonal expansion and differentiation. Following these steps, where do the majority of the newly generated differentiated effector T cells migrate to perform their protective functions?
a: They remain permanently in the lymphoid follicles
They enter the circulation and travel to the site of infection
They migrate immediately to the bone marrow for storage
They undergo mandatory apoptosis within the spleen
They convert into professional antigen-presenting cells
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.l: Following proliferation and differentiation in secondary lymphoid organs, effector T cells leave the lymph node via efferent lymphatics, enter the circulation, and migrate to peripheral sites of infection.
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.h: Think about where effector T cells need to go to fight active foreign pathogens in peripheral tissues.

8_cell_mediated_immune_response.q: A researcher treats a culture of naive T helper cells ($Th0$) with a combination of Interleukin-6 (IL-6) and Tissue Growth Factor-beta (TGF-beta). This specific cytokine milieu stimulates the expression of a key transcription factor, guiding the differentiation of these cells into a distinct functional subset. Which cytokine will these newly differentiated effector T cells characteristically secrete?
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.l: IL-6 and TGF-beta induce the differentiation of naive CD4+ T cells into the Th17 subset, which characteristically secretes Interleukin-17 (IL-17).
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.h: Identify the signature effector cytokine produced by the T-cell subset that is induced by IL-6 and TGF-beta.

8_cell_mediated_immune_response.q: A 44-year-old male is diagnosed with an infection caused by a facultative intracellular pathogen. Effector CD8+ Cytotoxic T Lymphocytes are deployed to destroy the infected host cells. The CTLs anchor firmly to the targets and execute a mechanism of cell-surface destruction mediated by the binding of a specific inducible T-cell surface ligand to a death receptor on the target cell. Which of the following pairs of molecules drives this specific apoptotic pathway?
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.l: In addition to perforin/granzyme exocytosis, activated CTLs express Fas Ligand (FasL), which binds to the Fas death receptor on target cells to directly activate the caspase cascade and induce apoptosis.
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.h: Look for the classic ligand-receptor pair responsible for the extrinsic pathway of programmed cell death (apoptosis).

8_cell_mediated_immune_response.q: A patient with a chronic parasitic worm infection shows a high concentration of alternatively activated macrophages (M2) in a tissue biopsy. Instead of driving inflammation and microbicidal destruction, these M2 cells are working actively to limit tissue damage, promote fibrosis, and initiate matrix remodeling. Which of the following combinations of substances is characteristically secreted by these M2 macrophages?
a: ROS, nitric oxide, and lysosomal enzymes
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.l: Alternatively activated macrophages (M2) are induced by IL-4 and IL-13 and function primarily in tissue repair, resolution of inflammation, and fibrosis by secreting growth factors and TGF-beta.
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.h: Identify the molecules that promote wound healing, fibrosis, and tissue repair rather than microbicidal destruction.

8_cell_mediated_immune_response.q: A 19-year-old male is infected with a strain of Herpes Simplex Virus (HSV). The virus attempts to remain completely hidden from adaptive cytotoxic detection inside host cells. To achieve this, the virus synthesizes a specialized peptide that binds to and directly interferes with the TAP (Transporter associated with antigen processing) protein complex. This viral evasion tactic will directly inhibit which of the following cellular processes?
a: The secretion of IL-2 from naive helper T cells
The processing and presentation of cytosolic antigens on MHC Class I
The activation of alternative M2 macrophages
The class-switching of B cells to IgE
The binding of LFA-1 to ICAM-1
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.l: HSV evades the immune system by producing a peptide that blocks the TAP transporter, which prevents the translocation of cytosolic viral peptides into the ER, halting MHC Class I antigen presentation.
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.h: Consider the exact role of the TAP transporter in cellular antigen processing and presentation.

8_cell_mediated_immune_response.q: An experimental model of viral infection evaluates the survival strategies of Cytomegalovirus (CMV). Investigators observe that CMV uses a multi-tiered strategy to completely neutralize host adaptive cell-mediated defenses. Specifically, CMV actively removes newly synthesized MHC Class I molecules from the endoplasmic reticulum. To prevent immediate destruction by host innate defenses due to this loss of MHC Class I, what does CMV do?
a: It completely downregulates all cell-surface activating ligands
It encodes a viral 'decoy' molecule that resembles MHC Class I to block NK cell attack
It secretes high concentrations of pro-inflammatory IL-12
It upregulates the transcription factor T-bet in host cells
It triggers immediate autocrine clonal expansion of Tregs
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.l: CMV downregulates MHC Class I expression to escape CD8+ CTLs, but to prevent being killed by NK cells (which scan for missing MHC I), it encodes a viral MHC Class I decoy protein to engage NK inhibitory receptors.
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.h: Think of how a virus can fool an NK cell's inhibitory receptors when real MHC Class I molecules are missing.

8_cell_mediated_immune_response.q: A 32-year-old female presents with an infection caused by an obligate intracellular pathogen. Her immune system initiates a cell-mediated response. During the initial antigen recognition stage in a regional lymph node, the naive T helper cell's CD4 coreceptor binds specifically to a ligand on the dendritic cell. Which of the following molecules serves as the specific binding site for this CD4 coreceptor?
a: MHC Class I molecule nonpolymorphic region
MHC Class II molecule nonpolymorphic region
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.l: The CD4 coreceptor on helper T cells binds specifically to the invariant, nonpolymorphic regions of MHC Class II molecules, stabilizing the interaction during antigen recognition.
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.h: Match the CD4 coreceptor to its corresponding class of MHC molecule on the APC surface.

8_cell_mediated_immune_response.q: An experimental drug is developed to block the development of allergic diseases. The drug works by binding to a specific transcription factor and completely halting the differentiation of naive T cells into the helper subset that secretes IL-4 and IL-5. Which master transcription factor is this drug targeting?
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.l: GATA3 is the master transcription factor required for the differentiation of naive CD4+ T cells into the Th2 effector subset, which secretes IL-4, IL-5, and IL-13.
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.h: Recall the master transcription factor that regulates the Th2 lineage.

8_cell_mediated_immune_response.q: A 21-year-old male is exposed to an extracellular fungus. The host immune system reacts by expanding a population of Th17 helper cells. These Th17 cells migrate to the barrier tissue and release their signature cytokines to clear the pathogen. Which of the following responses is the primary downstream function driven by these Th17 cytokines?
a: Classical activation of M1 macrophages to generate nitric oxide
Recruitment of neutrophils to drive localized extracellular inflammation
Activation of eosinophils to execute helminthic destruction
Suppression of self-reactive lymphocytes to prevent autoimmunity
Direct cell death via exocytosis of perforin and granzymes
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.l: The primary function of the Th17 subset is to protect against extracellular bacteria and fungi by recruiting neutrophils through the secretion of cytokines like IL-17.
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.h: Think of the specific leukocyte type that is strongly recruited by Th17 effector cells to clear fungi.

8_cell_mediated_immune_response.q: A molecular biologist modifies a line of professional antigen-presenting cells so that they are completely incapable of undergoing activation or expressing costimulators when exposed to microbial PAMPs. If these modified APCs attempt to present an antigen to a naive T lymphocyte, which of the following outcomes will most likely occur?
a: The naive T cell will proliferate excessively via autocrine IL-2
The naive T cell will fail to activate and may enter a state of non-responsiveness (anergy)
The naive T cell will differentiate immediately into a functional Th17 cell
The APC will undergo immediate apoptosis mediated by FasL
The T cell will switch its coreceptor expression from CD4 to CD8
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.l: If a naive T cell recognizes an antigen displayed on an APC but does not receive costimulatory signals (like CD28-B7 binding), it will fail to activate and can become functionally anergic.
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.h: Consider what happens to a naive T cell when Signal 1 is present but Signal 2 (costimulation) is missing.

8_cell_mediated_immune_response.q: A pathological review of a chronic granuloma from a patient with a long-standing intracellular infection reveals a high level of cross-regulation between different lymphocyte families. The investigators note that the high concentration of Interferon-gamma (IFN-gamma) present in the microenvironment is actively suppressing another helper branch. Which helper T cell subset's differentiation is directly inhibited by IFN-gamma?
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.l: Interferon-gamma (IFN-gamma), produced by Th1 cells, acts as a cross-regulatory signal that directly inhibits the proliferation and differentiation of Th2 cells.
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.h: Identify the helper T-cell subset whose development is suppressed by the signature Th1 cytokine, IFN-gamma.

8_cell_mediated_immune_response.q: A researcher isolates a group of professional antigen-presenting cells from the peripheral blood. Among them, she identifies a unique subset that does not require any prior activation or exposure to PAMPs to successfully present protein antigens to naive T lymphocytes. Which of the following APCs matches this description?
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.l: Dendritic cells are the most potent professional antigen-presenting cells and are uniquely capable of presenting antigen to and activating naive T cells without requiring prior activation.
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.h: Identify the primary professional APC that can activate naive T cells without needing to be pre-activated itself.

8_cell_mediated_immune_response.q: A 36-year-old male with an advanced tumor is being treated with a new experimental agent that blocks inhibitory pathways within the secondary lymphoid organs. The agent binds specifically to a cell surface receptor containing an inhibitory motif that normally recognizes the ligands PD-L1 and PD-L2. Which receptor is this drug targeting?
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.l: PD-1 is an essential inhibitory receptor expressed on activated T cells that contains an inhibitory motif and recognizes two structurally related ligands, PD-L1 and PD-L2.
8_cell_mediated_immune_response.h: Identify the T-cell coinhibitory molecule that pairs with the PD-L1 and PD-L2 ligands.

9_memory_tolerance.q: Immunologic tolerance induced when developing lymphocytes encounter self-antigens in primary lymphoid organs is called:
9_memory_tolerance.l: Central tolerance occurs in thymus and bone marrow.

9_memory_tolerance.q: Immunologic tolerance induced when mature lymphocytes encounter self-antigens in peripheral tissues is called:
9_memory_tolerance.l: Peripheral tolerance occurs in secondary lymphoid organs and tissues.

9_memory_tolerance.q: Which process allows self-reactive B cells to change their antigen specificity?
9_memory_tolerance.l: Receptor editing is unique to B cells during central tolerance.

9_memory_tolerance.q: Anergy in T cells is best defined as:
9_memory_tolerance.l: Anergic T cells survive but fail to respond to antigen.

9_memory_tolerance.q: T-cell anergy commonly occurs when antigen is recognized without:
9_memory_tolerance.l: Lack of costimulation induces T-cell anergy.

9_memory_tolerance.q: Which inhibitory receptor promotes T-cell anergy?
9_memory_tolerance.l: CTLA-4 inhibits T-cell activation.

9_memory_tolerance.q: Another inhibitory receptor involved in peripheral tolerance is:
9_memory_tolerance.l: PD-1 suppresses T-cell responses.

9_memory_tolerance.q: Positive selection ensures that mature T cells are:
9_memory_tolerance.l: Positive selection establishes MHC restriction.

9_memory_tolerance.q: Negative selection primarily ensures:
9_memory_tolerance.l: Negative selection eliminates autoreactive T cells.

9_memory_tolerance.q: Failure of negative selection most likely results in:
9_memory_tolerance.l: Autoreactive T cells escape deletion.

9_memory_tolerance.q: Approximately what percentage of thymocytes survive thymic selection?
9_memory_tolerance.l: Less than 5% survive positive and negative selection.

9_memory_tolerance.q: Positive selection occurs primarily in the:
9_memory_tolerance.l: Positive selection occurs in the cortex.

9_memory_tolerance.q: Negative selection occurs primarily in the:
9_memory_tolerance.l: Negative selection occurs in the medulla.

9_memory_tolerance.q: MHC restriction means T cells recognize antigens only when presented by:
9_memory_tolerance.l: T cells require antigen presentation on self-MHC.

9_memory_tolerance.q: Which protein allows thymic expression of tissue-restricted antigens?
9_memory_tolerance.l: AIRE promotes expression of peripheral antigens in thymus.

9_memory_tolerance.q: Mutation of AIRE causes:
9_memory_tolerance.l: AIRE deficiency impairs negative selection.

9_memory_tolerance.q: Regulatory T-cell development depends on which transcription factor?
9_memory_tolerance.l: FoxP3 is the master regulator of Tregs.

9_memory_tolerance.q: Regulatory T cells are primarily:
9_memory_tolerance.l: Tregs are a specialized subset of CD4+ T cells.

9_memory_tolerance.q: Which cytokine is required for generation and maintenance of Tregs?
9_memory_tolerance.l: IL-2 is essential for Treg survival.

9_memory_tolerance.q: FoxP3 mutation results in:
9_memory_tolerance.l: IPEX syndrome is caused by FoxP3 mutation.

9_memory_tolerance.q: A characteristic feature of IPEX syndrome is:
9_memory_tolerance.l: IPEX commonly presents with eczema.

9_memory_tolerance.q: Another hallmark feature of IPEX syndrome is:
9_memory_tolerance.l: Autoimmune endocrine disease is common in IPEX.

9_memory_tolerance.q: Which cells suppress activation of potentially harmful self-reactive lymphocytes?
9_memory_tolerance.l: Tregs maintain self-tolerance.

9_memory_tolerance.q: Strong binding of immature B cells to self-antigens in bone marrow leads to:
9_memory_tolerance.l: Self-reactive B cells undergo clonal deletion.

9_memory_tolerance.q: Only B cells with what affinity for self-antigens progress to maturity?
9_memory_tolerance.l: Low self-reactivity allows survival.

9_memory_tolerance.q: The first antibody produced during a primary immune response is:
9_memory_tolerance.l: IgM is the earliest antibody produced.

9_memory_tolerance.q: During a primary immune response, IgM is followed by:
9_memory_tolerance.l: Class switching generates IgG and other isotypes.

9_memory_tolerance.q: The secondary immune response is primarily due to:
9_memory_tolerance.l: Memory cells mediate faster responses.

9_memory_tolerance.q: Compared with the primary response, the secondary response is:
9_memory_tolerance.l: Memory cells accelerate the response.

9_memory_tolerance.q: Which antibody predominates in the secondary immune response?
9_memory_tolerance.l: IgG is the dominant antibody in secondary responses.

9_memory_tolerance.q: The effectiveness of vaccine boosters is based on:
9_memory_tolerance.l: Boosters activate memory cells.

9_memory_tolerance.q: Secondary antibodies generally have:
9_memory_tolerance.l: Affinity maturation increases antibody affinity.

9_memory_tolerance.q: Higher affinity antibodies in secondary responses are due to:
9_memory_tolerance.l: Somatic hypermutation drives affinity maturation.

9_memory_tolerance.q: Memory B cells typically have a lifespan of approximately:
9_memory_tolerance.l: Memory B cells can persist for about 10 years.

9_memory_tolerance.q: Memory T cells can persist for:
9_memory_tolerance.l: Memory T cells may last decades.

9_memory_tolerance.q: The primary immune response usually has what antibody affinity?
9_memory_tolerance.l: Primary responses generate lower-affinity antibodies.

9_memory_tolerance.q: Class switching is most dependent on:
9_memory_tolerance.l: Protein antigens require T-cell help for class switching.

9_memory_tolerance.q: The major mechanism of peripheral tolerance involving functional silencing is:
9_memory_tolerance.l: Anergy renders lymphocytes nonresponsive.

9_memory_tolerance.q: Which mechanism of peripheral tolerance physically removes self-reactive lymphocytes?
9_memory_tolerance.l: Deletion eliminates autoreactive cells.

9_memory_tolerance.q: Peripheral tolerance can also be mediated by:
9_memory_tolerance.l: Tregs suppress self-reactive immune responses.

9_memory_tolerance.q: Self-reactive lymphocytes that escape central tolerance are mainly controlled by:
9_memory_tolerance.l: Peripheral tolerance prevents autoimmunity.

11_immunization.q: Which type of immunity occurs when the body produces its own antibodies after exposure to an antigen?
11_immunization.l: Active immunity develops when the individual produces their own immune response.
11_immunization.h: Think: your body makes the antibodies.

11_immunization.q: Which type of immunity is produced by administering preformed antibodies?
11_immunization.l: Passive immunity results from receiving antibodies made elsewhere.
11_immunization.h: Think: antibodies are given, not made.

11_immunization.q: What is the main advantage of passive immunity?
11_immunization.l: Passive immunity provides rapid protection because antibodies are already present.

11_immunization.q: What is the major disadvantage of active immunity?
11_immunization.l: Active immunity requires time for the immune system to respond.

11_immunization.q: Which immunoglobulin crosses the placenta?
11_immunization.l: IgG provides passive immunity to the fetus.

11_immunization.q: Which immunoglobulin is present in breast milk?
11_immunization.l: Secretory IgA protects mucosal surfaces in the neonate.

11_immunization.q: Natural active immunity is best acquired through:
11_immunization.l: Natural active immunity follows infection and recovery.

11_immunization.q: Artificial active immunity refers to:
11_immunization.l: Vaccination stimulates active immune responses.

11_immunization.q: Which of the following is an example of artificial passive immunity?
11_immunization.l: Antivenom contains preformed antibodies.

11_immunization.q: Which immunity involves both antibodies and T cells?
11_immunization.l: Active immunity generates both humoral and cellular responses.

11_immunization.q: Rabies post-exposure prophylaxis consists of:
11_immunization.l: Rabies requires both passive and active immunization.

11_immunization.q: Hepatitis B post-exposure prophylaxis consists of:
11_immunization.l: HBIG provides immediate protection while vaccine induces immunity.

11_immunization.q: Tetanus post-exposure prophylaxis consists of:
11_immunization.l: Both active and passive immunization are used.

11_immunization.q: A tetanus booster is generally recommended after how many years?
11_immunization.l: Booster is recommended if more than 10 years have elapsed.

11_immunization.q: Live attenuated vaccines contain:
11_immunization.l: Live attenuated organisms can still replicate.

11_immunization.q: Live attenuated vaccines generally induce:
11_immunization.l: Live vaccines closely mimic natural infection.

11_immunization.q: Which vaccine is live attenuated?
11_immunization.l: MMR is a classic live attenuated vaccine.

11_immunization.q: Which polio vaccine is live attenuated?
11_immunization.l: Sabin vaccine is the oral live attenuated polio vaccine.

11_immunization.q: Which vaccine induces mucosal IgA?
11_immunization.l: Oral Sabin vaccine induces intestinal IgA.

11_immunization.q: Which vaccine is inactivated (killed)?
11_immunization.l: Rabies vaccine is inactivated.

11_immunization.q: Which polio vaccine induces serum IgG?
11_immunization.l: Injected Salk vaccine produces serum IgG.

11_immunization.q: Which vaccine is safer in immunocompromised patients?
11_immunization.l: Killed vaccines cannot replicate and are safer.

11_immunization.q: Which vaccine type carries a risk of reversion to virulence?
11_immunization.l: Live vaccines can rarely revert to pathogenic forms.
11_immunization.h: Reason Sabin fell out of favor.

11_immunization.q: Killed vaccines primarily induce:
11_immunization.l: Killed vaccines mainly stimulate antibody production.

11_immunization.q: Killed vaccines usually require:
11_immunization.l: Their weaker immune response requires boosters.

11_immunization.q: Which vaccine is recombinant?
11_immunization.l: HBV vaccine uses recombinant surface antigen.

11_immunization.q: The Hepatitis B vaccine contains:
11_immunization.l: HBsAg is used in the recombinant vaccine.

11_immunization.q: Toxoids are:
11_immunization.l: Toxoids retain antigenicity but lose toxicity.
